sections.title: What to test
items: All 8 pairs on one screen (B2 Unit1 ex.G)
Multi-life loss for multiple wrong pairs

exercises.title: G: Matching (Stative verbs)
exercises.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap a numbered beginning, then tap the matching ending letter.

items.sentence: We think **B** that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.
items.answer: B – that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: "We think" collocates with ending B: "that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.".
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: Read the beginning and ending together — only one ending forms a natural, grammatical sentence.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: We think that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.

wrongOptions.H: Ending H ("his grandfather — same build, same walk.") does not collocate naturally with "We think".
wrongOptions.F: Ending F ("to the school library, not to me.") does not collocate naturally with "We think".
wrongOptions.G: Ending G ("three sets of batteries and a charger.") does not collocate naturally with "We think".
wrongOptions.A: Ending A ("slightly less than I did before I started the diet.") does not collocate naturally with "We think".
wrongOptions.D: Ending D ("wonderful in the early morning.") does not collocate naturally with "We think".
wrongOptions.E: Ending E ("a great deal to the local residents.") does not collocate naturally with "We think".
wrongOptions.C: Ending C ("whether to change careers at my age.") does not collocate naturally with "We think".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not force ending H ("his grandfather — same build, same walk.") — only B completes "We think" naturally.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Say the full sentence aloud — if it sounds unnatural, try a different ending letter.

items.sentence: The young athlete resembles **H** his grandfather — same build, same walk.
items.answer: H – his grandfather — same build, same walk.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: "The young athlete resembles" collocates with ending H: "his grandfather — same build, same walk.".
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: Read the beginning and ending together — only one ending forms a natural, grammatical sentence.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The young athlete resembles his grandfather — same build, same walk.

wrongOptions.B: Ending B ("that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.") does not collocate naturally with "The young athlete resembles".
wrongOptions.F: Ending F ("to the school library, not to me.") does not collocate naturally with "The young athlete resembles".
wrongOptions.G: Ending G ("three sets of batteries and a charger.") does not collocate naturally with "The young athlete resembles".
wrongOptions.A: Ending A ("slightly less than I did before I started the diet.") does not collocate naturally with "The young athlete resembles".
wrongOptions.D: Ending D ("wonderful in the early morning.") does not collocate naturally with "The young athlete resembles".
wrongOptions.E: Ending E ("a great deal to the local residents.") does not collocate naturally with "The young athlete resembles".
wrongOptions.C: Ending C ("whether to change careers at my age.") does not collocate naturally with "The young athlete resembles".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not force ending B ("that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.") — only H completes "The young athlete resembles" naturally.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Say the full sentence aloud — if it sounds unnatural, try a different ending letter.

items.sentence: That dictionary belongs **F** to the school library, not to me.
items.answer: F – to the school library, not to me.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: "That dictionary belongs" collocates with ending F: "to the school library, not to me.".
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: Read the beginning and ending together — only one ending forms a natural, grammatical sentence.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: That dictionary belongs to the school library, not to me.

wrongOptions.B: Ending B ("that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.") does not collocate naturally with "That dictionary belongs".
wrongOptions.H: Ending H ("his grandfather — same build, same walk.") does not collocate naturally with "That dictionary belongs".
wrongOptions.G: Ending G ("three sets of batteries and a charger.") does not collocate naturally with "That dictionary belongs".
wrongOptions.A: Ending A ("slightly less than I did before I started the diet.") does not collocate naturally with "That dictionary belongs".
wrongOptions.D: Ending D ("wonderful in the early morning.") does not collocate naturally with "That dictionary belongs".
wrongOptions.E: Ending E ("a great deal to the local residents.") does not collocate naturally with "That dictionary belongs".
wrongOptions.C: Ending C ("whether to change careers at my age.") does not collocate naturally with "That dictionary belongs".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not force ending B ("that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.") — only F completes "That dictionary belongs" naturally.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Prepositional phrases are fixed chunks — small changes (on/at/for) change the meaning completely.
